title: Emotional Intelligence: Why It Can Matter More Than IQ
subtitle: The groundbreaking book that launched the EQ revolution — arguing that self-awareness, empathy, impulse control, and social skill matter more than raw intellect for success in life.
author: Daniel Goleman
themes: The two-mind model: rational vs emotional brain
Emotional hijacking and the amygdala's role
Self-awareness as the keystone of emotional competence
Empathy as a fundamental social skill
The plasticity of emotional circuits through neuroplasticity
The cost of emotional illiteracy across relationships, health, and work
awards: New York Times Best Seller (1.5 years on list)
TIME Magazine 25 Most Influential Business Management Books
excerpt: IQ is not enough. Self-awareness, impulse control, empathy, and social deftness — emotional intelligence — matter more for success in relationships, work, and health than the analytical skills measured by IQ tests. And these skills can be taught.
keyIdeas: The emotional brain can hijack the rational brain — the amygdala triggers impulses before the neocortex can deliberate
Self-awareness — knowing one's own emotions as they occur — is the cornerstone of emotional intelligence
Empathy builds on self-awareness: the ability to read others' feelings is essential for social competence
Emotional skills can be learned and strengthened through neuroplasticity at any age
keyTakeaways: IQ accounts for at most 20% of life success; the rest depends on emotional and social competencies
Impulse control — delaying gratification — predicts better outcomes than IQ in longitudinal studies
Emotional literacy programs in schools reduce violence, anxiety, and improve academic performance
Marital satisfaction depends more on emotional repair skills than on intelligence or shared interests
whoShouldRead: Educators and parents wanting to understand how emotional skills shape child development
Managers and leaders seeking the evidence base for soft skills in the workplace
Anyone curious about the neuroscience behind emotions and why some smart people fail
Therapists and counselors looking for a broad framework connecting emotion, cognition, and behavior
whoShouldSkip: Readers already familiar with the EQ concept from the past 25 years of cultural saturation
Those seeking strict academic science — Goleman's pop-science approach prioritizes accessibility over rigor
Anyone expecting a step-by-step emotional skills training manual rather than a survey of research
